title: St. John Climacus, St. Samson of Ireland, St. Mary of Egypt & St. Zosimas
narrative:
St. John Climacus of Sinai, St. Samson of Dol, St. Mary of Egypt, and St. Zosimas the Priest-Monk

On the thirtieth and final day of Megabit, the holy Church closes the Lenten month with one of the most luminous assemblies in the entire Ethiopian Synaxarium: four witnesses whose lives together constitute the complete programme of the spiritual ascent that the Great Fast has been designed to enable. Saint John Climacus — John of the Ladder — is the supreme theorist of that ascent: the abbot of Sinai whose Ladder of Divine Ascent, written at the request of his community and dedicated to the abbot of the neighbouring monastery, describes in thirty steps the progressive movement of the human soul from slavery to the passions through the stages of asceticism, discernment, and prayer to the summit of the three theological virtues, faith, hope, and love. The thirty steps of the Ladder correspond in the Ethiopian tradition's reading to the thirty days of Megabit: the community that has kept the Great Fast has been ascending the ladder, and John Climacus on the final day of the month is the guide who points to the step they have reached and shows the steps that still remain.

Saint Samson of Dol is this day's great geographical discovery: a Welsh monk and bishop of the sixth century who trained in the monastic school of Llantwit Major in Wales, received his formation in the most austere traditions of Celtic monasticism, and then crossed the sea to Cornwall, to the Scilly Isles, and finally to Brittany in France, where he founded the monastery of Dol that became the centre of Breton Christianity. His missionary journeys, conducted entirely on foot and by boat, covered a vast arc from Wales to Brittany to Cornwall to Ireland and back, planting communities of prayer in landscapes as remote and challenging as any desert. The Ethiopian tradition honours Samson as a representative of the Celtic monastic tradition whose islands and promontories were the desert of the northern seas — the place where the same Spirit that drove Christ into the Judean wilderness drove the Irish and Welsh monks into the cold Atlantic.

Saint Mary of Egypt is among the most beloved figures in the entire Oriental hagiographical tradition and the great penitential saint of the Lenten season: a woman who had spent seventeen years in sexual licence in Alexandria, who was prevented by an invisible force from entering the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem on the feast of the Exaltation of the Cross, who cried out in desperation to the Theotokos whose icon she saw in the narthex, and who was suddenly flooded with the awareness of the wretchedness of her life and the possibility of its transformation. She spent the next forty-seven years alone in the desert beyond the Jordan, enduring extraordinary physical and interior suffering, her body ravaged by the desert heat and cold, her soul slowly purified of the passions that had governed her Alexandrian years. She was found by the priest-monk Zosimas in her final year, and she asked him to return the following year with the Eucharist, which he did, finding her dead on the ground beside a text she had written in the sand requesting burial.

Saint Zosimas the Priest-Monk is the fourth and essential figure of this closing assembly: not because his own holiness equals Mary's — the tradition is clear that the great penitent of the Jordan surpassed him — but because without him Mary's story would never have been told and her witness would have been lost. Zosimas is the priest who brings the Eucharist to the solitary, who buries the dead penitent, who carries her story back to his community. He is the representative of the institutional church in its proper relationship to the extraordinary mystic: not presiding over her experience but serving it, providing the sacramental structure that undergirds even the most solitary and extreme forms of the spiritual life. John Climacus ascending the ladder of virtues, Samson sailing the northern seas, Mary weeping in the Jordan desert, Zosimas bringing the Eucharist to the dying penitent: the month of Megabit closes, the Great Fast approaches its end, and the community that has traversed its thirty days is handed now to Holy Week and the Paschal mystery that awaits beyond it. Amen.
